topics.topic_title: Introduction to Art, Crafts and Design

sub_topics.title: Art Awareness and Appreciation

teacher_steps: Defining Art (performing, literary and visual)
Categorising branches of Art: Visual Art, Performing Arts, Digital Art...
Visiting different places where the named branches of Art are found: Museum, cultural centres...
Critiquing various artworks
learner_steps: Participate in defining different forms of Art
Categorize branches of Art through group activities

sub_topics.short_notes: Art is categorized into three main branches: Visual Arts (painting, sculpture, crafts), Performing Arts (dance, music, drama), and Literary Arts (poetry, prose). Digital Art is an emerging contemporary form.

vocabulary.definition: Art forms that create works which are primarily visual in nature, such as painting, drawing, sculpture, and crafts
Art forms involving performance, such as dance, music, and drama
Artistic work or practice that uses digital technology as part of the creative process

questions: List and explain the three main branches of Art
Give examples of artworks in each branch
Explain the difference between Visual and Performing Arts

sub_topics.title: Careers in Art and Design
competences: Classify careers in Art and Design

teacher_steps: Identifying careers in Art and Design
Classifying careers: Art Teacher, fabric designer, curator...
Matching artworks to specific categories of art: Graphic designer, fine artists...

sub_topics.short_notes: Art and Design careers span multiple fields including education (Art Teacher), museums (Curator), commercial design (Graphic Designer, Fashion Designer), fine arts (Painter, Sculptor), and applied arts (Fabric Designer, Interior Decorator).

vocabulary.definition: A professional who manages and organizes art collections in museums or galleries
A professional who creates visual content for communication and marketing

questions: Name five careers in Art and Design
Classify art careers into categories (education, commercial, fine arts)
Explain the role of a curator in the art field

sub_topics.title: Visual Arts

teacher_steps: Identifying forms of Visual arts: Fine Arts, Crafts, Design
Discussing the functions of Visual arts: income generation, identity, cultural heritage...
Analysing forms of visual arts
learner_steps: Identify different forms of Visual Arts

sub_topics.short_notes: Visual Arts encompass Fine Arts (painting, drawing, printmaking), Crafts (pottery, weaving, basketry), and Design (graphic design, fashion design, interior design). These serve multiple functions including cultural preservation, economic value, and aesthetic expression.

vocabulary.definition: Art created primarily for aesthetic purposes rather than functional use, including painting, sculpture, and drawing
Skilled activities involving making things by hand, such as pottery, weaving, and woodwork

questions: Distinguish between Fine Arts and Crafts
List three functions of Visual Arts in society
Give examples of Design in Visual Arts

sub_topics.title: Pioneers of Zambian Visual Arts
competences: Identify pioneers of Zambian Visual Arts

teacher_steps: Identifying pioneers of Zambian Visual Arts: Henry Tayali, Gabriel Ellison, Cynthia Zukas, Akwila Simpasa, Godfrey Setti, Shadreck Simukanga, Martin Abasi Phiri...
Critiquing works of the pioneer visual artists

sub_topics.short_notes: Zambian Visual Arts pioneers include Henry Tayali (known for expressive paintings), Gabriel Ellison (sculptor and painter), Cynthia Zukas (painter), Akwila Simpasa (sculptor), and others who established the foundation of contemporary Zambian art.

vocabulary.definition: An artist who is among the first to develop or establish an art movement or style in a particular region
An artist who works in visual mediums such as painting, sculpture, or drawing

questions: Name five Zambian pioneer visual artists
Describe the contribution of Henry Tayali to Zambian art
Analyze the work of one pioneer Zambian artist

sub_topics.title: Elements of Art
competences: Apply Art elements in practical activities

teacher_steps: Identifying elements of Art: Line, shape, space, value, texture, tone and colour
Experimenting with lines to express and communicate ideas
Using lines to create contour, gesture drawings and calligraphy writing
Analysing the use of shape, form and space
Creating Artworks with the use of shape, form and space
learner_steps: Practice using different types of lines
Create artworks using shapes and forms
Apply texture and value in drawings

sub_topics.short_notes: The seven elements of Art are the building blocks of visual composition: Line (marks that span a distance), Shape (enclosed areas), Form (3D objects), Space (area around objects), Value (lightness/darkness), Texture (surface quality), and Color (hue, value, intensity).

vocabulary.definition: A mark with length and direction, used to create contours, patterns, and textures
A two-dimensional enclosed area defined by boundaries
A three-dimensional object with height, width, and depth
The lightness or darkness of a color or tone
The surface quality of an object, either actual (tactile) or visual (implied)

questions: Name and define the seven elements of Art
Create a drawing demonstrating at least four elements of Art
Explain how line can be used to express emotions in artwork

sub_topics.title: Principles of Art
competences: Create Artworks using principles of art

teacher_steps: Defining Art principles: balance, contrast, unity, proportion, emphasis, rhythm, pattern, visual movement...
Discussing use of Art principles as applied in Artworks
Creating an Artwork showing prominent use of one or more of the Art principles
learner_steps: Critique use of principles in peer work

sub_topics.short_notes: Art Principles are guidelines for organizing elements of art: Balance (visual weight distribution), Contrast (differences in elements), Unity (harmonious relationship), Proportion (relative size), Emphasis (focal point), Rhythm (repetition creating movement), Pattern (repeated elements), and Movement (visual path through artwork).

vocabulary.definition: The distribution of visual weight in an artwork, can be symmetrical, asymmetrical, or radial
The difference between elements, such as light and dark or rough and smooth
The sense that all parts of an artwork work together to create a harmonious whole
The part of an artwork that catches the viewer's attention first
The repetition of elements to create a sense of organized movement

questions: Define five principles of Art
Create an artwork demonstrating balance and emphasis
Analyze how a famous artwork uses art principles

sub_topics.title: Basic Shapes
competences: Apply knowledge of basic shapes in drawing

teacher_steps: Identifying basic shapes: cuboid, pyramid, cone, rectangular box...
Using basic shapes to draw
learner_steps: Use shapes to construct complex objects

sub_topics.short_notes: Basic shapes in art include 2D shapes (circle, square, triangle, rectangle) and 3D forms (sphere, cube, cylinder, cone, pyramid). Understanding these fundamental shapes helps in constructing and drawing complex objects by breaking them down into simpler components.

vocabulary.definition: A three-dimensional rectangular box shape with six faces
A three-dimensional shape with a polygon base and triangular faces meeting at an apex
A three-dimensional shape with a circular base and curved surface tapering to a point

questions: Draw and label five basic 3D shapes
Use basic shapes to construct a drawing of a house
Explain how basic shapes help in drawing complex objects

sub_topics.title: Types of Shading

teacher_steps: Discussing various shading techniques: pointillism, stippling, hatching, cross-hatching, smudging, scribbling, smash...
Applying tonal gradation on drawings
Applying various shading techniques
learner_steps: Apply shading to create form and depth
Create a value scale from light to dark

sub_topics.short_notes: Shading techniques create the illusion of form, depth, and light in drawings. Common techniques include: Hatching (parallel lines), Cross-hatching (intersecting lines), Stippling (dots), Pointillism (controlled dots), Scribbling (random lines), Smudging (blending), and Blending (smooth gradations).

vocabulary.definition: A shading technique using parallel lines to create tonal values
A shading technique using intersecting sets of parallel lines to create darker tones
A shading technique using small dots to create tonal values
The gradual transition from light to dark values

questions: Demonstrate three different shading techniques
Create a value scale showing gradation from light to dark
Apply appropriate shading to give form to a drawn sphere

sub_topics.title: Different Textures
competences: Compose pictures using a variety of textures and values

teacher_steps: Defining the Art elements: texture and value
Identifying different textures: actual and visual texture
Exploring a variety of textures, value and shading
Composing pictures using a variety of textures and values
learner_steps: Distinguish between actual and visual texture
Create texture samples using various techniques

sub_topics.short_notes: Texture refers to the surface quality of an object. Actual texture (tactile) can be felt by touch, while visual texture (implied) is created through artistic techniques to suggest how a surface might feel. Value works with texture to create depth and interest in artwork.

vocabulary.definition: The physical, tactile surface quality that can be felt by touch
The illusion of texture created through artistic techniques like shading and pattern

questions: Explain the difference between actual and visual texture
Create a texture chart showing five different textures
Compose a drawing using various textures and values

sub_topics.title: Prehistoric Rock Art of Zambia
competences: Research the Prehistoric Rock Art of Zambia

teacher_steps: Identifying the prehistoric rock art of Zambia: Mwela rocks, Kasama rock arts...
Researching on the prehistoric arts of Zambia: Materials and tools, themes, styles of painting...
Analysing the prehistoric rock Art of Zambia

sub_topics.short_notes: Zambian prehistoric rock art includes important sites like Mwela Rocks and Kasama rock paintings. These artworks provide insights into early human life, beliefs, and activities. Common themes include hunting scenes, animal representations, and geometric patterns created using natural pigments.

vocabulary.definition: Art created before written records, typically through painting or carving on rocks
Images created on natural rock surfaces, including paintings and engravings

questions: Name two important prehistoric rock art sites in Zambia
Describe the materials and techniques used in prehistoric rock art
What themes are common in Zambian prehistoric rock art?

sub_topics.title: Traditional Crafts

teacher_steps: Identifying the traditional crafts: reed mats, stools, mortar and pestle...

sub_topics.short_notes: Traditional Zambian crafts include functional and decorative items such as reed mats, wooden stools, mortars and pestles, baskets, pottery, and wood carvings. These crafts reflect cultural heritage and practical needs of Zambian communities.

vocabulary.definition: Handmade objects created using techniques passed down through generations
Craftwork designed primarily for practical use

questions: List five traditional Zambian crafts
Explain the cultural significance of traditional crafts
Describe the making process of one traditional craft

sub_topics.title: Post-independence and Contemporary Art

teacher_steps: Explaining post-independence and Contemporary Art
learner_steps: Compare traditional and contemporary art forms

sub_topics.short_notes: Post-independence Zambian art (from 1964) saw the emergence of formal art education and institutions. Contemporary art reflects modern themes, techniques, and global influences while maintaining Zambian identity and cultural expressions.

vocabulary.definition: Art created after Zambia gained independence in 1964, often reflecting national identity
Art created in the present period, reflecting current ideas, techniques, and social issues

questions: Explain the development of Zambian art after independence
How does contemporary Zambian art differ from traditional art?
Name two contemporary Zambian artists and their work

sub_topics.title: Traditional Decorations

teacher_steps: Making traditional decorations: Hand prints, jewellery...
learner_steps: Make traditional jewelry using local materials

sub_topics.short_notes: Traditional Zambian decorations include hand prints, beadwork, jewelry, body paintings, and scarification patterns. These decorations serve ceremonial, social, and aesthetic purposes in various Zambian cultures.

vocabulary.definition: Ornamental designs and adornments following cultural traditions
Decorative work created using beads, often with cultural significance

questions: Create a traditional decoration using local materials
Explain the cultural significance of traditional decorations
Describe three types of traditional Zambian decorations

sub_topics.title: Traditional Artefacts

teacher_steps: Creating traditional Artefacts: Basketry, weaving, pottery, beadwork...

sub_topics.short_notes: Traditional Zambian artefacts include baskets (for storage and carrying), woven mats, pottery (for cooking and storage), beadwork (for decoration and ceremony), and carved wooden items. These represent practical skills and cultural heritage.

vocabulary.definition: An object made by human hands, especially one of cultural or historical interest
The craft of making baskets and woven containers
Objects made from clay and hardened by heat

questions: Create a traditional artefact using local materials
Explain the function of traditional artefacts in Zambian culture
Demonstrate one traditional craft technique

sub_topics.title: Types of Colours

teacher_steps: Experimenting colours of the spectrum: primary, secondary and tertiary
learner_steps: Mix primary colors to create secondary colors
Create a color wheel showing relationships

sub_topics.short_notes: The color spectrum includes Primary colors (red, blue, yellow - cannot be created by mixing), Secondary colors (orange, green, violet - created by mixing two primaries), and Tertiary colors (created by mixing primary and secondary colors).

vocabulary.definition: The three basic colors (red, blue, yellow) that cannot be created by mixing other colors
Colors created by mixing two primary colors (orange, green, violet)
Colors created by mixing a primary and a secondary color
The range of colors visible to the human eye

questions: Name the three primary colors
Mix and demonstrate how to create secondary colors
Create a color wheel showing primary, secondary, and tertiary colors

sub_topics.title: Colour Schemes

teacher_steps: Using colour schemes in the painting: Monochromatic, Analogous, Tertiary, Complementary, Colour triads, Split complementary...
learner_steps: Practice each color scheme in small studies
Create paintings using specific color schemes
Analyze color schemes in famous artworks

sub_topics.short_notes: Color schemes are harmonious combinations of colors: Monochromatic (variations of one color), Analogous (adjacent colors on wheel), Complementary (opposite colors), Tertiary (three evenly spaced tertiary colors), Triadic (three evenly spaced colors), Split-complementary (one color plus two adjacent to its complement).

vocabulary.definition: A color scheme using variations of a single color
Colors that are adjacent to each other on the color wheel
Colors that are opposite each other on the color wheel
Three colors evenly spaced on the color wheel

questions: Define and demonstrate three color schemes
Create a painting using a complementary color scheme
Identify the color scheme used in a given artwork
